texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Biogas is generated in anaerobic conditions by microorganisms from organic substrates. It consists of a gas mixture, composed mainly of methane and carbon dioxide. Biogas is produced in large scale in sewage treatment plants, land fi lls and anaerobic digestion plants (Niemczewska, 2012). Its chemical composition varies as a function of the substrate type and operational conditions (Ryckebosch et al., 2011).
texts.text: Biogas is generated in anaerobic conditions by microorganisms from organic substrates. It consists of a gas mixture, composed mainly of methane and carbon dioxide. Biogas is produced in large scale in sewage treatment plants, land fi lls and anaerobic digestion plants (Niemczewska, 2012). Its chemical composition varies as a function of the substrate type and operational conditions (Ryckebosch et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Amongst the possible uses of the produced biogas, puri fi cation for injection into the natural gas grid is known as the most interesting option from an economic point of view. The use of biogas as a fuel is also advantageous for the environment, because methane is a greenhouse gas approximately 20 times more aggressive than carbon dioxide (Dirkse, 2007). After puri fi cation, biogas is called biomethane and has typically 95 e 97% of CH4 and 1 e 3% of CO2 (Ryckebosch et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas permeation has great potential for the biogas upgrading. The present work evaluates the performance of membranes made of two different polymers aiming at the carbon dioxide (CO2) removal: cellulose acetate and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). The results indicated that CO2 caused membrane plasticization, which led to lower separation factors in tests with gas mixtures, in comparison to the pure gas experiments. Based on experimental results, a simpli fi ed model was developed which considered the permeability variation with the CO2 partial pressure in the gas feed. The modeling approach for a single stage process pursued a compromise between methane (CH4) purity and recovery. For a given purity, the highest recoveries were obtained for the cellulose acetate membrane, due to its higher selectivity. For this membrane, a CH4 purity of 97% led to a recovery of 86.8%, while the PDMS membrane presented a CH4 recovery of only 19.8%, considering the same CH4 content. A multiple stage design improved methane recovery considerably in comparison to the single stage process for both membranes, making it possible to attain 99.6% CH4 purity associated with 99.8% CH4 recovery for the cellulose acetate membrane.
texts.text: Membrane gas permeation has great potential for the biogas upgrading. The present work evaluates the performance of membranes made of two different polymers aiming at the carbon dioxide (CO2) removal: cellulose acetate and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). The results indicated that CO2 caused membrane plasticization, which led to lower separation factors in tests with gas mixtures, in comparison to the pure gas experiments. Based on experimental results, a simpli fi ed model was developed which considered the permeability variation with the CO2 partial pressure in the gas feed. The modeling approach for a single stage process pursued a compromise between methane (CH4) purity and recovery. For a given purity, the highest recoveries were obtained for the cellulose acetate membrane, due to its higher selectivity. For this membrane, a CH4 purity of 97% led to a recovery of 86.8%, while the PDMS membrane presented a CH4 recovery of only 19.8%, considering the same CH4 content. A multiple stage design improved methane recovery considerably in comparison to the single stage process for both membranes, making it possible to attain 99.6% CH4 purity associated with 99.8% CH4 recovery for the cellulose acetate membrane.

texts.orig: A membrane separation process is an alternative to address these drawbacks. Among the membrane technologies available for biogas upgrading, gas permeation is the most widely used as it has already been developed for industrial scale applications. It was fi rst established in the 1980 ' s for CO2 removal from natural gas. The separation is based on the sorption-diffusion mechanism using dense membranes, for which there is a compromise between selectivity and permeability.

texts.orig: Many polymeric or inorganic materials can be used in membrane manufacturing for CO2/CH4 separation. However, in industrial scale, only polymeric materials have been used, mainly due to their low cost (Basu et al., 2010). texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the main challenges in high pressure membrane separation is plasticization. By de fi nition, this is a pressure dependent phenomenon, caused by the dissolution of certain substances in the polymeric matrix. Plasticization causes swelling and increases segmental mobility of polymeric chains. In gas separation membranes, swelling induced by CO2 affects process performance and increases the probability of material failure. For example, the selectivity reduction when processing a gas mixture in comparison to the pure gases data is attributed to plasticization. Plasticization occurs typically in glassy polymers, leading to a decrease in the glass transition temperature by reducing the interaction between adjacent segments of polymeric chains (Ismail and Lorna, 2002). The magnitude of plasticization depends on the quantity of sorbed gas.

texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas mixtures used in the experiments were prepared online using CH4 (99,995%, Linde AG) and CO2 (99,99%, Linde AG). Gas permeation tests were performed using pure gases and gas mixtures containing 20, 40, 50, 60 and 80% of CO2. Differential pressure between feed and permeate varied from 6 to 16 bar.
texts.text: Gas mixtures used in the experiments were prepared online using CH4 (99,995%, Linde AG) and CO2 (99,99%, Linde AG). Gas permeation tests were performed using pure gases and gas mixtures containing 20, 40, 50, 60 and 80% of CO2. Differential pressure between feed and permeate varied from 6 to 16 bar.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two membranes were tested: a composite commercial cellulose acetate membrane and a dense fi lm of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). The dense fi lm was prepared using a polymeric solution (RTV615A e Momentive) and a cross-linking agent (RTV615B e Momentive) in the proportion of 10:1.
texts.text: Two membranes were tested: a composite commercial cellulose acetate membrane and a dense fi lm of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). The dense fi lm was prepared using a polymeric solution (RTV615A e Momentive) and a cross-linking agent (RTV615B e Momentive) in the proportion of 10:1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The tests with pure gases and mixtures were carried out in an automatized system whose schematic diagram is presented in Fig. 1. Gas composition and fl ow rate were adjusted by the mass fl ow controllers MFC-1 and MFC-2. Feed pressure was controlled by the valve VSP-1. The three permeation cells (C1, C2 and C3) could be operated independently, allowing up to three tests running simultaneously sharing the same feed gas. An isothermal bath whose liquid fl owed through the exterior of the cells controlled the temperature during the experiments. The permeation cells operated with membrane areas in the order of 10 cm 2 . The permeate collection system was equipped with valves VS-3 to VS-13, pressure sensors PI-2, PI-3 and PI-4, a vacuum pump and the carrier gas, Helium. In the experiments using gas mixtures, gas composition was determined using an online gas chromatograph (MicroCG CP 4900, Varian). The permeate pressure increase with time was measured continuously during the experiments, starting from vacuum.

texts.text: Two con fi gurations were proposed for the process simulation, using single and multiple stages permeation. The schematics of both con fi gurations are presented in Fig. 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The multiple stages system is composed by three stages. The CH4 concentrate stream from the fi rst stage is sent to the second one, whose permeate is recycled to the feed stream and its retentate corresponds to the fi nal stream of puri fi ed biogas. The permeate stream from the fi rst stage is sent to an additional pass, in order to reduce the loss of CH4. The additional pass recycles its retentate to the feed stream and the permeate constitutes the fi nal stream, enriched in CO2.

texts.orig: One relevant simpli fi cation imposed to the multiple stage simulation was that the molar composition of the recycle stream should be equal to the one where it was being recycled to. This condition was applied for the recycle of the second stage as well as for the additional pass. The model was implemented using the Python programming language.

texts.orig: The variation of permeate pressure with respect to time, as showed in Fig. 4 for the cellulose acetate membrane at 25  C and feed pressure of 6 bar, was obtained for each gas permeation experiment. The graph indicates that permeate pressure started at vacuum and increased during the test, due to gas mixture permeation through the membrane. The graph clearly shows that the gas mixture composition has in fl uenced the pressure slope over time. The highest slope occurred for pure CO2, whereas the smallest one was observed for pure CH4. This behavior makes it clear that the membrane is selective for CO2. For CO2 and CH4 mixtures, intermediate slopes were observed in comparison to the pure gases: mixtures containing a higher CO2 concentration permeated faster.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5(a) shows CO2 permeance for the cellulose acetate membrane as a function of CO2 composition in the feed mixture, for three feed pressures (6, 11 and 16 bar). When pure gas was fed to the membrane, CO2 permeance value was higher than what was observed when using mixtures. Besides, as CO2 composition in the mixture increases, the permeance to this gas also increases. This might be explained by the CO2 swelling effect on the membrane material. This effect was intensi fi ed when increasing feed pressure. Thus, permeabilities were higher at 11 and 16 bar when compared to permeabilities at 6 bar. Indeed, other authors such as ETTOUNEY and MAJEED (Ettouney and Majeed, 1997) observed that CO2 permeability was affected by its percentage in the mixture. The competition among permeating species in the mixture resulted in lower CO2 permeability, when compared to the pure gas results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CH4 permeance as a function of CO2 composition is presented in Fig. 5(b) for the cellulose acetate membrane. Its permeance also increased for higher concentrations of CO2 in the mixture. Accordingly, the minimal permeability is found for pure CH4. CH4 permeation was favored by mixing it with CO2 due to the plasticizing effect. As observed for CO2, the experiments most affected by the swelling effect were the ones with the higher contents of CO2 in feed mixture and the higher feed pressures (11 and 16 bar). HOUDE et al. (Houde et al., 1996) also studied cellulose acetate membranes permeabilities to CH4, CO2 and their mixtures and veri fi ed that there was an enhancement in the permeation of the slower species (CH4) in the mixtures, caused by plasticization.

texts.orig: Selectivity as a function of CO2 composition in the mixture is shown in Fig. 6 for the cellulose acetate membrane at each of the three tested feed pressures. Selectivity values reported for 0% of CO2 corresponds to the ideal selectivity. Higher ideal selectivities were obtained in more elevated feed pressures due to the plasticization effect, which increased the CO2 permeability with little effect on the CH4 permeability. DONOHUE et al. (Donohue et al., 1989) also observed a very signi fi cant increase of CO2 permeability with pressure, which resulted in higher ideal selectivities at higher pressures. The order of magnitude of the pressures tested by this author is similar to the present work, and ideal selectivities were also situated between 30 and 60.

texts.orig: Graphs were obtained from PDMS membrane experiments for CO2 permeability as a function of the mixture composition, as shown in Fig. 7(a). Feed pressures of 6 and 16 bar were applied. The data shows that there was almost no variation of CO2 permeability with regard to pressure. This conclusion agrees with ETTOUNEY and MAJEED (Ettouney and Majeed, 1997), who stated that the permeation of pure gases and gas mixtures shows very little dependence with feed pressure in elastomeric membranes such as PDMS. However, a slight increase in permeability was observed for the CO2 as its concentration in the mixture increased, indicating the occurrence of some degree of plasticization.

texts.orig: The selectivity, which was calculated using the results of CO2 and CH4 permeabilities, is presented in Fig. 8 as a function of mixture composition. Comparing Fig. 8 with Fig. 6, it is possible to observe that PDMS selectivity is much lower than the cellulose acetate one. The ideal selectivity for PDMS was about 4.5, whereas for cellulose acetate it was above 30. PDMS selectivity also decreased as the feed pressure and CO2 content increased, but not so expressively. The drop in PDMS selectivity was a consequence of the fact that its permeability to CH4 increased at a higher rate than its permeability to CO2. This was due to plasticization, as previously discussed for cellulose acetate.

texts.orig: The results for a single stage presented a compromise between CH4 purity and recovery, as can be seen in Fig. 10. As membrane area increases, so does CH4 purity. Nevertheless, this reduces CO2 concentrate partial pressure, which increases CH4 transportation through the membrane. Because of this effect, there is a drop in the CH4 recovery as the membrane area increases. In other words, in single stage systems, the more the concentrate fl ow is enriched in CH4, the more CH4 is lost in the permeate fl ow.

As pressure drop estimated between the outlet and the entry of each stage was not signi fi cant, feed and concentrate pressures were considered constant and equal to 16 bar along each stage. A comparison for single and multiple stage systems is shown in Table 2. The multiple stage modeling estimated a purity of 99.6% associated with a recovery of 99.8% for the cellulose acetate membrane, which can be considered an excellent performance. The membrane area estimated in this case was of 29 m 2 . The simulation for PDMS estimated a purity of 97.0% and a recovery of 79.2%, for a membrane area of 36 m 2 . Therefore, the multiple stage process signi fi cantly enhanced CH4 recovery in comparison to the single stage process. The inconveniences associated with this process are the increased complexity, the requirement of a new compression stage and the need of higher membrane areas.

texts.orig: 6. Conclusions
texts.text: 6. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the gas permeation experiments, cellulose acetate membrane presented higher CO2/CH4 selectivity in comparison to PDMS. The results for both materials indicated that mixture composition interferes with membrane plasticization: the higher the CO2 content in the mixture, the higher the plasticization, leading to higher permeabilities for both CO2 and CH4. Plasticization resulted in selectivity reduction, mainly for the cellulose acetate membrane, which is a vitreous polymer. CO2/CH4 selectivity for mixtures in this membrane was situated between 8 and 16, whereas the ideal selectivity was more than 30 for all tested feed pressures.
texts.text: In the gas permeation experiments, cellulose acetate membrane presented higher CO2/CH4 selectivity in comparison to PDMS. The results for both materials indicated that mixture composition interferes with membrane plasticization: the higher the CO2 content in the mixture, the higher the plasticization, leading to higher permeabilities for both CO2 and CH4. Plasticization resulted in selectivity reduction, mainly for the cellulose acetate membrane, which is a vitreous polymer. CO2/CH4 selectivity for mixtures in this membrane was situated between 8 and 16, whereas the ideal selectivity was more than 30 for all tested feed pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The modeling for a single stage process allowed the evaluation of CH4 purity along the permeation module. The single stage process was characterized by the compromise between methane purity and recovery. As membrane area was increased, methane purity also increased. However, this caused CO2 partial pressure in the concentrate to decrease, making it easier for CH4 to be transported through the membrane. For a given purity, the highest recoveries were obtained for the cellulose acetate membrane, due to its higher selectivity. For the cellulose acetate membrane, a methane purity of 97% corresponded to a recovery of 86.8%. For the PDMS membrane the recovery, in this case, was of only 19.8%.

The single stage process was characterized by the compromise between methane purity and recovery. As membrane area was increased, methane purity also increased. However, this caused CO2 partial pressure in the concentrate to decrease, making it easier for CH4 to be transported through the membrane. For a given purity, the highest recoveries were obtained for the cellulose acetate membrane, due to its higher selectivity. For the cellulose acetate membrane, a methane purity of 97% corresponded to a recovery of 86.8%. For the PDMS membrane the recovery, in this case, was of only 19.8%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The multiple stage process improved methane recovery considerably in comparison to the single stage process for both membranes. Therefore, it can be concluded that it is possible to produce biomethane from biogas using commercially available gas permeation membranes minimizing CH4 loss through the correct process design, even considering the high plasticization effect of the carbon dioxide on the glassy polymer. However, a more rigorous modeling and the process optimization should be carried out.
